note.fr: <ph id='montaigne'>Montaigne</ph> a lu et annoté le <w id='on-the-nature-of-things-lucretius'>De rerum natura</w>, dont il parsème les <w id='essays-montaigne'>Essais</w> de citations. Sa reprise du <c>naturalisme</c> de <ph id='lucretius'>Lucrèce</ph> est sélective : il en retient surtout la <c>mort</c>, la condition charnelle et le refus du dogmatisme, plus qu'un système. La lecture est directe, mais la filiation reste indirecte, faite d'emprunts littéraires sans magistère reconnu.
note.en: <ph id='montaigne'>Montaigne</ph> read and annotated <w id='on-the-nature-of-things-lucretius'>On the Nature of Things</w>, which he laces with quotations throughout the <w id='essays-montaigne'>Essays</w>. His uptake of <ph id='lucretius'>Lucretius</ph>'s <c>naturalism</c> is selective: he draws above all on <c>death</c>, the bodily condition and the refusal of dogmatism, rather than on a system. The reading is direct, yet the filiation stays indirect, made of literary borrowings without acknowledged mastership.

sources.fr: L'exemplaire du De rerum natura annoté de la main de Montaigne (notes marginales conservées)
sources.en: Montaigne's hand-annotated copy of On the Nature of Things (extant marginalia)

sources.fr: Citations de Lucrèce parmi les plus fréquentes des Essais, surtout sur la mort et la condition charnelle
sources.en: Among the most frequent quotations in the Essays, especially on death and the bodily condition
